dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for unknown problem/person down in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Dizziness and nausea for about an hour now.
I see; can you tell me your name and how old you are?
Good, let me check your responsiveness. What is today's date?
I think it's the 22nd... October?
Great, can you tell me where you are right now?
I'm in my home in [CITY].
Your eyes are opening on their own-thank you for being alert and cooperative.
And can you tell me your full name, please?
Mary... Mary Thompson.
Could you demonstrate moving both arms for me, Mary?
Of course, here you go-can I cross them too to show movement?
Your breathing seems regular. Are you having any trouble breathing currently?
I'm not having much trouble, no. Breathing is fine so far.
Ma'am, I'll take your pulse and oxygen level now, just to be thorough.
Respirations are 18, and oxygen saturation at 97% via percutaneous method-good levels for now.
Pulse is 103, regular, and strong-normal signs from what we see so far.
Blood pressure is 200 over 110-slightly high, but stable for the moment.
Pulse is 103-still steady and within normal rates for your age and condition.
Let me check your respiratory rates again. It remains at 18 breaths a minute, consistent with undistressed state.
Rechecking the blood pressure-confirms 200 over 110. We will monitor this closely during transport.
Lung sounds are clear on both sides. Chest rise is equal-no signs of deep pain or respiratory retching. Would you like to take a temperature just in case?
Can I take a small blood sample for glucose? It's just to ensure no hypoglycemia is contributing to the dizziness-better safe than sorry.
Oxygen saturation is consistent at 97%, mirrored by your pulse. No drops in O2 levels when we checked once again-good.
Your temperature via the ear is 98.6. No fevers, and you look as though you are in a well-controlled condition for now.
Your skin is pink, warm, and very dry-no signs of edema or major bleeding. A strong radial pulse supports circulation status, even with high blood pressure readings so far.
How long have you been feeling this dizziness? Was it gradual or sudden?.
It started an hour ago while I was out in the living room. I had no warning-it just struck suddenly, with no previous signs except slight spinning.
Have you experienced chest pain, shortness of breath, or anything similar alongside this incident?.
No-no chest pain. It doesn't feel like pain at all, just intense spinning and some nausea, without any shortness of breath that I could tell. No cough or diarrhea either.
Do you have any allergies-drugs or foods?.
No known drug allergies or food intolerances based on past history..
What prescriptions are you currently on at the moment?.
Zyrtec, meclizine, and albuterol. These manage allergies, dizziness, and asthma, respectively..
What have you had treated medically in your history?.
I have asthma and vertigo-both rated as mild by my primary. Meclizine helps most of the time, but not today..
Was the last time you had food prior to the dizziness?.
Yes, she was eating dinner in the room right before we called..
How exactly did the dizziness come on? Tell me the story again so I can sync it with possible causes, if you don't mind sharing details..
It came on when I stood up. I felt spinning and immediately called 911. It didn't respond to meclizine this time-terrible when that happens..
When did this first start for you today-can you confirm the exact time or activity involved?.
About 4 o'clock-that's when the dizziness and spinning began..
Does this or the dizziness get worse with a specific movement, position, or action?.
Definitely when I stand-like vertigo episodes, only heavier..
What one word would you use to describe the dizziness-classification-wise is just for our note..
Spinning. Like being on a ride or losing balance suddenly..
Does it spread to another part of your body, like your arms or the back of your head?.
No-it stays in the center..
On a scale from 1 to 10, how much does the dizziness bother you?.
Maybe 6-there's no pain, more like an intense sensation..
Since it's started at 4, has it gone up, down, or stayed the same?.
It's the same. No changes right now..
I did drink alcohol today. I also have some seasonal allergies which can flare up this time of year..
Skin is pink and dry. No signs of trauma, edema, fever, or cough. No signs of respiratory distress or bruising anywhere detected during exam..
Would you like to do a FAST exam with me to make sure everything is well?.
Sure, if needed. Can I move my face or eyes freely during the test?.
Of course-can you smile, raise your eyebrows, and then close your eyes tightly with no trouble?.
I'll try-here it is, all at once if you'd like to see..
Good. Try to lift both arms up and keep them there, can you?.
Let's see… I can do it-no numbness or drop..
You're doing well. You pass the FAST exam. No signs of facial droop, arm drift, or slurred speech-no neurological concerns at the moment..
I'll place you on the cardiac monitor now, to get a baseline and check for any irregular rhythm or ST elevation, just so we are in full complacency..
Chest movements are normal, with equal rise and fall on both sides. No abnormal sounds or audible wheezing-lungs appear to be healthy..
No dysrhythmias or ectopy noted on EKG as we interpret it during baseline-which is reassuring in patients with vertigo and past asthma..
First 4-lead ECG was completed and all good so far. We'll proceed with a 12-lead to ensure full coverage and accuracy in monitoring cardiac activity..
First 12-lead ECG is now taken and shows nothing-negative for ST elevation or other cardiac anomalies..
EKG rhythm shows a clear sinus with no ST elevation-consistent with a very typical and healthy heart beat for our situation..
Now I will start an IV for stability and possible medication access if you need further support. Will be going in your left arm-it's the IV's ideal spot in this case..
IV is in place, good, and steady. Finger vein worked perfectly on the first try-short and painless for you..
According to protocol and current clinical status, we will proceed with a standard medicinal dose of Zofran (ondansetron) to help control the nausea and offer some relief for your condition..
That was 4 mg ondansetron administered via the IV. It should begin to help in one of two minutes once it's circulated a bit more..
Let me directly check vitals-initial response seems good; no concerns from baseline values yet..
Pulse has decreased now to 78, still regular. Slower but stable, which is a good sign given the current medication..
Blood pressure is now at 171 over 93-still elevated but trending down, which is encouraging..
Oxygen saturation remains at 100 percent. Great take on the O₂ level and reduced distress during the medication..
Blood glucose, however, showed no reading-possibly due to an error in the sample, but that will be confirmed in the hospital..
Your GCS score is at 15, showing full awareness and clarity. We're treating under the medical nausea/vomiting protocol today. Have you noticed any changes since your condition was documented for us?.
I feel a little better than before-no fever, no chest pain. Still dizzy, but a lot of pressure is gone, oddly enough..
Assessment is non-urgent-we'll transport you to the hospital for further care and 12-lead review..
We'll be heading directly to [COMPANY_NAME] for handling and emergency evaluation-your care team there is on standby..
I'll secure you to the stretcher now for safety and comfort during transport, no matter the ride's smoothness..
You're all under control now-ready for transport. No danger, and no further intervention is needed at this stage..
Consent is yours. I trust you guys with the machine and care..
Let me briefly recheck vitals again before the road trip starts. No sudden drops expected, but better not to assume anything..
Repeat IV position and medication cross-check done earlier-standards are well protected and followed..
I'll quickly reassess the dizziness-since you mentioned no pain, we will ensure no change in symptoms while you're on the stretcher..
Reviewing your S.A.M.P.L.E. information once more for accuracy and to capture any clinically relevant missed behaviors..
Stretcher is secured and the vehicle will be ready in no time. No movements have been noted until transport initiation with our manual assistance..
Transportation is complete and your patient is now being moved to triage in [CITY]. Everything is clear, with no EMS issues pending-only compliance on both sides..
In-person report given to the ER RN at arrival. Just confirming no changes occurred in your mental orientation or clinical stability from the field..

All assessments complete and handed over to the ER. Patient showed no signs of chest pain, shortness of breath, or slurred speech. No fever or cough were noted, and there's also no evidence of diarrhea at this time..
Pain levels remain at 0 as documented, and the GCS reading stays anticipated clear at 15 (Ox4), so no neurological alarm at the time of transport..
Second 12-lead ECG confirms negative results-still sinus with no suspected complications of ST or dysrhythmia events-nothing elevated clinically..
ECG has been re-reviewed twice for quality control and nurse review. Negative for ectopy and no diagnostic changes from previous passes..
Stretcher is secured, fully monitored during transport. No patient movement or incident observed for review..
Patient was transferred with support from stretcher to base hospital's chair, medically safe for care turnover and in a stable, controlled state..
Team is leaving the scene with proper PPE for hygiene and safety. No patient complaints or updates on conditions before departure are to be noted on this leg of the call..
Final disposition is confirmed as non-urgent transport per protocol for medical nausea/vomiting..
Initial 12-lead showed potential STEMI concerns, but they resolved to nonspecific. No ongoing chest pain, no classification elevated-confirmed in procedure notes from EPCR..
You're set, care and support have transferred. Hospital will give you continuing attention with no immediate life threat..
Team is leaving the scene with proper PPE for hygiene and safety. No patient complaints or updates on conditions before departure are to be noted on this leg of the call..
